@graph.name: Land Rover Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 Era Map
Land Rover Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 306DT engine typically costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only depending on specification. A complete supply and fit package for a 3.0 TDV6 long engine is approximately £7,610 including VAT for models up to 2015, including a 12‑month warranty. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 V6 diesel delivers excellent torque and refinement, but it has a well‑documented weakness: crankshaft and bearing failure. The narrow bearing design can cause fatigue and oil starvation. Extended oil change intervals accelerate wear. With strict 5,000‑mile oil changes and proper servicing, the risk can be managed, but it remains a known vulnerability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most serious issue is sudden crankshaft failure — typically without warning — caused by the narrow bearing design. Other common problems include turbocharger failure (wastegate actuator on SDV6), oil leaks (valve cover, seals), HPFP failure, EGR valve issues, and DPF clogging.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the Range Rover's high retained value (typically £25,000–£60,000+), a £4,250–£6,500 reconditioned engine is a modest fraction of the vehicle's worth. Replacement is almost always financially viable. Even at the higher end, it rarely exceeds our 60% Land Rover threshold against current market values.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover's engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0 V6 diesel uses engine code 306DT (also 30DDTX). This is stamped on the engine block and listed on your V5C. TDV6 variants are single‑turbo (211‑258 bhp); SDV6 variants are twin‑turbo (245‑306 bhp). The badge on the tailgate will confirm which you have.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a TDV6 / SDV6 engine issue, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most issues — turbochargers, EGR valves, oil leaks, fuel pumps — are repairable. However, if the crankshaft fails or bearings spin, the only reliable fix is a full long engine replacement. Rebuilding the bottom end is rarely cost‑effective once a knock or oil pressure drop is detected.

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: Insurance jobs; warranty claims; full provenance

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Range Rover L405 TDV6/SDV6 generation. A complete supply and fit package for a 3.0 TDV6 long engine is approximately £7,610 including VAT for models up to 2015, including a 12‑month warranty.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Range Rover TDV6/SDV6 engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent Land Rover specialist).

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover TDV6/SDV6 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Crankshaft Bearing Failure (The "Knock of Death")
problems.symptoms: Sudden loud knocking from the engine, oil pressure warning light, complete engine seizure — often without prior warning.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking or see oil pressure drop, stop driving immediately.

problems.recommendation: This is the single most serious issue with the 306DT. The narrow crankshaft bearing design can cause fatigue and oil starvation, leading to sudden catastrophic failure. JLR issued a 10‑year/120,000‑mile extended warranty on crankshaft failure for certain models (2012–2015). The single most important preventative measure is a strict 5,000‑mile oil change interval — JLR's 21,000‑mile "long life" intervals are considered by specialists to be a significant contributor to premature failure. At the first sign of knocking, stop driving immediately and have the engine assessed — rebuild is rarely cost‑effective.

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure (Wastegate Actuator / Turbo Unit)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive smoke, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, engine warning light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored turbo failure can send debris into the engine).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you notice power loss or unusual noises.

problems.recommendation: The 306DT's turbocharger(s) are a known failure point, particularly on the twin‑turbo SDV6. The wastegate actuator can fail or seize, causing overboost or underboost conditions. If the turbo fails catastrophically, debris can be ingested into the engine, causing further damage. Regular oil changes and allowing the engine to idle after hard driving (to cool the turbo) are essential preventative measures.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover / Oil Filter Housing / Crank Seal)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil leak.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored oil loss can lead to engine damage).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leak is detected.

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are common on the 306DT. The valve cover can warp and leak — the plastic cover is prone to cracking. The oil filter housing may also leak. Given the engine's known bearing vulnerability, maintaining correct oil level is critical. If you notice oil spots under your Range Rover or a burning oil smell, investigate immediately — oil starvation is one of the primary triggers of crankshaft failure.

problems.title: High‑Pressure Fuel Pump (HPFP) Failure (Bosch CP4.2)
problems.symptoms: Hard starting, rough running, fuel in oil, engine warning light, sudden engine shutdown.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught early; ignored HPFP failure can contaminate the entire fuel system).

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if hard starting or rough running occurs.

problems.recommendation: The Bosch CP4.2 high‑pressure fuel pump is a known weak point across many diesel platforms — the 306DT is no exception. The CP4.2 is lubricated by the fuel itself, and poor fuel quality or water contamination can cause internal failure. If the pump fails, metal debris can be sent through the entire fuel system, requiring an expensive system flush. Regular fuel filter changes and using high‑quality diesel are essential.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Full engine replacement — rebuild rarely cost‑effective
Worth fixing unless engine is already compromised
Requires fuel system flush — worth it on a good vehicle

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Land Rover service history with documented 5,000‑mile oil changes
* JLR crankshaft extended warranty still in place (2012–2015 models)
* Evidence of turbo servicing and regular fuel filter changes
* Warranty remaining or an extended warranty in place
avoidIf: * No evidence of regular oil changes (5,000‑mile intervals) — this is critical
* Any sign of knocking or oil pressure warning
* Poor service history — these engines demand strict maintenance
* High‑mileage SDV6 with no turbo history — twin turbo failure is expensive

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Range Rover TDV6 and SDV6 offer effortless torque and refined V6 performance, but they come with a well‑documented vulnerability: crankshaft bearing failure. This is the "squeeze‑zone" flagship variant for a reason — the combination of refinement and reliability risk defines this badge. Buy on service history above all else. A vehicle with documented 5,000‑mile oil changes and JLR's extended crankshaft warranty is a safe bet; a neglected example is a financial time bomb. As one specialist put it: "Always buy the service history, not the engine code — the best TDV6 is one that's been properly maintained."

cta.label: → Compare Range Rover TDV6/SDV6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover TDV6 / SDV6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Any knocking? Any smoke? (knock = stop immediately)
2. Service History — 5,000‑mile oil changes? This is non‑negotiable
3. Crankshaft Warranty — Is the 10‑year/120,000‑mile JLR extended warranty active?
4. Oil Leaks — Check under the vehicle and around the valve cover
5. Turbo Health — Any whistling, lag, or smoke on acceleration?
6. Oil Condition — Any fuel in oil? (indicates HPFP issue)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any HPFP or turbo codes?
8. DPF Regeneration — Has the vehicle done regular longer runs?
